planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty, humus-enriched soil in partial to full shade in spring; auriculas prefer cool conditions and excellent drainage — heavy, wet soil in summer is fatal.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, gritty, slightly alkaline to neutral, humus-enriched soil in partial to full shade; auricula (mountain cowslip) is a European alpine primrose that has been cultivated for centuries as a specialist exhibition plant, with growers producing flowers of extraordinary complexity — perfectly circular, velvet-textured petals in yellow, red, blue-gray, maroon, and white, many with intricate patterns of contrasting colors and a distinctive white or yellow central 'paste' (meal) that coats the center of the flower like powdered sugar. It requires excellent drainage (rot easily in soggy soil), cool temperatures, and consistent moisture. The thick, fleshy, powder-coated (farinose) leaves are distinctive. A heritage plant with centuries of breeding history, prized by specialists in the UK but underused in American gardens. Best in cool climates; struggles in hot, humid summers south of zone 7.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; yellow loosestrife spreads aggressively by rhizomes in moist, fertile conditions and is best used where spreading can be contained or accommodated.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; yellow loosestrife is a robust European garden perennial producing whorled, lance-shaped leaves and cheerful, bright yellow, cup-shaped flowers held in loose spikes from early to midsummer — an excellent, long-lasting yellow for the partially shaded border, particularly in moist conditions where many perennials struggle. The plant spreads freely by rhizomes in fertile, moist soil and can colonize large areas in garden borders; edge annually to control spread or plant in a contained bed. Excellent for stream banks, pond margins, and rain garden edges. 'Alexander' (creamy-gold variegated leaves) and 'Golden Alexander' are popular selections that are slightly less vigorous than the straight species. Not to be confused with the invasive purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria), which is a different genus.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; yellow foxglove is a true perennial unlike the common biennial foxglove and returns reliably each year without needing to self-sow to perpetuate.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; yellow foxglove is a true perennial (unlike the common biennial D. purpurea) native to European woodlands, producing tall spikes of tubular, pale creamy-yellow flowers with light brown veining and spotting inside in early to midsummer — a refined and elegant foxglove in a soft color that pairs beautifully with blue salvias, geraniums, and hostas in a shade border. The plant is reliably long-lived and returns for many years without needing to reseed itself. All parts are toxic, as with all foxgloves. Self-sows in suitable conditions. Excellent in combination with the biennial common foxglove (D. purpurea) for a continuous succession of foxglove interest. Tolerates more shade than most other foxgloves.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun in spring; Elliott's lovegrass is tolerant of heat, drought, and salt spray, making it ideal for coastal and hot, dry southern landscapes.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, sandy to loamy, poor to average soil in full sun; Elliott's lovegrass (blue lovegrass) is a native southeastern US ornamental grass with unusually attractive blue-gray to silver-blue foliage — the narrow, arching leaves have a distinctly glaucous quality that is rare among warm-season grasses and provides cool, silvery-blue contrast in the summer landscape. Open, delicate panicles of tiny, silvery-purple spikelets appear in summer and fall, creating a misty, translucent effect when backlit. The plant is highly adaptable to the difficult coastal and sandy soils of the Gulf South, tolerating salt spray, drought, and poor soil conditions with negligible care. A natural choice for low-water, native-plant, and coastal landscape designs in zones 7–10. Does not become as weedy as some lovegrass species.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; bat-faced cuphea is one of the most continuously blooming and hummingbird-attractive annuals available for Gulf South summer gardens.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; bat-faced cuphea (bat's wing cuphea) is a Mexican native grown for its unique and vivid flowers — small, tubular, with brilliant red petals at the base and a pair of purple-red, rounded, ear-like upper petals that give each flower the uncanny appearance of a tiny bat's face, displayed in dense clusters from late spring until frost. The flowers are irresistible to ruby-throated hummingbirds. Plants bloom essentially nonstop in warm weather with no deadheading required. Extremely heat-tolerant and productive in Gulf Coast summer conditions when most cool-season annuals have declined. In zones 9–11 it overwinters as a semi-woody subshrub; in cooler climates grow as a long-season annual. 'Georgia Scarlet' is a popular selection.
